statusLine: Americas crude markets remain tight with WTI at $95/bbl and commercial inventories declining sharply across all products amid ongoing Middle East supply route disruptions.
fullAnalysis:
US crude markets show continued tightness with commercial crude stocks falling 7.1 million barrels in the week ending April 24, bringing inventories to 397.9 million barrels. This drawdown coincides with sustained US production at 13.6 million barrels per day, indicating strong demand pull despite high output levels. The Strategic Petroleum Reserve remains at historically low levels of 78.8 million barrels, providing limited buffer capacity.

WTI crude gained 1.69% week-on-week to $95.43/bbl in the week ending April 24, while the WTI-Brent spread widened to $16.04, reflecting the outsized impact of Middle East supply disruptions on international markets versus Americas-focused benchmarks. This spread suggests Americas crude is finding relative value despite elevated absolute price levels, though retail gasoline prices climbed $0.079 to $4.257/gallon, pressuring US consumers.

Critical maritime advisories remain in effect across multiple Middle East chokepoints, with Houthi attacks in the Red Sea and Bab el Mandeb Strait creating severe disruptions to global tanker traffic. Iranian threats in the Strait of Hormuz compound supply route risks, forcing longer voyage routes that tighten available tanker capacity and elevate freight costs for crude imports to Americas refineries.

Product inventories show broad-based tightness with gasoline stocks down 6.1 million barrels to 222.3 million barrels and distillates falling 4.5 million barrels to 103.6 million barrels. The simultaneous decline across crude and products suggests strong refinery runs are unable to keep pace with robust demand, particularly concerning given the approaching driving season. While diesel prices eased $0.052 to $5.351/gallon, gasoline price increases signal emerging supply-demand imbalances that could intensify through summer months.
keyPoints: US commercial crude inventories fell 7.1 million barrels to 397.9 million barrels in week ending April 24, indicating tight market conditions
WTI gained 1.69% week-on-week to $95.43/bbl while WTI-Brent spread widened to $16.04, showing Americas crude relative resilience
Multiple critical maritime advisories active for Red Sea, Persian Gulf chokepoints creating global supply chain disruptions
Gasoline inventories dropped 6.1 million barrels with retail prices up $0.079 to $4.257/gallon ahead of driving season
US production steady at 13.6 million bpd but unable to offset inventory draws across all product categories
Strategic Petroleum Reserve remains depleted at 78.8 million barrels, limiting emergency response capacity
